raw_title: The Presumed Prevalence/Persuasiveness of Online Misinformation and Americans’ Dissatisfaction With Democracy
raw_abstract: American public discourse about online misinformation often exaggerates its prevalence and persuasiveness, potentially harming Americans’ evaluations of democratic governance. This project uses two studies conducted during the 2020 and 2022 elections to test whether the presumed prevalence/persuasiveness of online misinformation (P3OM)—the perception that online misinformation is both widespread and persuasive—undermines Americans’ satisfaction with democracy. Fixed-effects regression analysis of an eight-wave online panel survey reveals that both dimensions of P3OM are correlated with reduced democratic satisfaction. A follow-up online survey experiment provides evidence that exposure to messages about online misinformation reduces democratic satisfaction. Theoretical implications and strategies for reducing the presumed influence of online misinformation on democratic satisfaction are discussed.
title_en: The Presumed Prevalence/Persuasiveness of Online Misinformation and Americans’ Dissatisfaction With Democracy
title_zh: 在线虚假信息的假定普遍性/说服力与美国人对其民主制度的不满
abstract_en: American public discourse about online misinformation often exaggerates its prevalence and persuasiveness, potentially harming Americans’ evaluations of democratic governance. This project uses two studies conducted during the 2020 and 2022 elections to test whether the presumed prevalence/persuasiveness of online misinformation (P3OM)—the perception that online misinformation is both widespread and persuasive—undermines Americans’ satisfaction with democracy. Fixed-effects regression analysis of an eight-wave online panel survey reveals that both dimensions of P3OM are correlated with reduced democratic satisfaction. A follow-up online survey experiment provides evidence that exposure to messages about online misinformation reduces democratic satisfaction. Theoretical implications and strategies for reducing the presumed influence of online misinformation on democratic satisfaction are discussed.
abstract_zh: 美国公众关于在线虚假信息的讨论常常夸大其普遍性和说服力，这可能损害美国人对民主治理的评价。本项目利用2020年和2022年选举期间进行的两项研究，测试在线虚假信息的假定普遍性/说服力（P3OM）——即认为在线虚假信息既广泛存在又具有说服力的观念——是否会削弱美国人对民主的满意度。对一项八波在线面板调查的固定效应回归分析显示，P3OM的两个维度都与民主满意度降低相关。一项后续在线调查实验提供了证据，表明接触关于在线虚假信息的信息会降低民主满意度。本文讨论了理论意义以及减少在线虚假信息对民主满意度的假定影响的策略。
plain_summary_zh: 这项研究发现，当人们认为网上虚假信息很常见且容易让人相信时，他们对自己国家的民主制度会更不满意。研究通过调查和实验证明，这种观念会降低人们对民主的满意度。
plain_summary_en: This study found that when people believe online misinformation is common and persuasive, they become less satisfied with their country's democracy. Research using surveys and experiments shows this perception reduces democratic satisfaction.
research_direction_zh: 探索公众对虚假信息的认知如何影响政治态度和民主制度评价，特别是在数字媒体环境下的心理机制和社会后果。
research_direction_en: Investigating how public perceptions of misinformation affect political attitudes and evaluations of democratic systems, particularly the psychological mechanisms and social consequences in digital media environments.
